questions.question_html:
Shabana wants to put a lace border all around a rectangular table cover

3 m long and 2 m wide. Find the length of the lace required by Shabana.
questions.solution_html:
Length of the rectangular table cover = 3 m
Breadth of the rectangular table cover = 2 m
Therefore, the length of the lace required will be equal to the perimeter of the rectangular table cover.
Now, the perimeter of the rectangular table cover
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (length + breadth) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (3 m + 2 m) = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 m = 10 m
So, length of the lace required is 10 m.

questions.question_html: An athlete takes 10 rounds of a rectangular park, 50 m long and 25 m wide. Find the total distance covered by him.
questions.solution_html:
Length of the rectangular park = 50 m
The breadth of the rectangular park = 25 m
The total distance covered by the athlete in one round will be the perimeter of the park.
Now, the perimeter of the rectangular park
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (length + breadth)= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (50 m + 25 m)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 75 m = 150 m
So, the distance covered by the athlete in one round is 150 m.
Thus, distance covered in 10 rounds = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 150 m = 1500m
Therefore, the total distance covered by the athlete is 1500 m.

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of a rectangle whose length and breadth are 150 cm and 1 m respectively.
questions.solution_html:
Given:
Length = 150 cm
Breadth = 1m = 100 cm
Therefore, Perimeter of the rectangle
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (length + breadth)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (150 cm + 100 cm)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (250 cm)
= 500 cm = 5 m

questions.question_html: A farmer has a rectangular field of length and breadth 240 m and 180 m respectively. He wants to fence it with 3 rounds of rope as shown in the figure. What is the total length of rope he must use?
questions.solution_html:
The farmer has to cover three times the perimeter of that field.
Thus, the total length of rope required is thrice its perimeter.
Perimeter of the field = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (length + breadth)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} ( 240 m + 180 m)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 420 m = 840 m
Therefore, Total length of rope required = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 840 m = 2520 m

questions.question_html: Find the cost of fencing a rectangular park of length 250 m and breadth 175 m at the rate of ₹12 per metre.
questions.solution_html:
Given:
Length of the rectangular park = 250 m
Breadth of the rectangular park = 175 m
To calculate the cost of fencing we require perimeter.
Therefore, Perimeter of the rectangle = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (length + breadth)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (250 m + 175 m)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (425 m) = 850 m
Cost of fencing 1m of park = ₹12
Thus, the total cost of fencing the park
= ₹12 {tex}\times{/tex} 850 = ₹10200

questions.question_html: Find the distance travelled by Shaina if she takes three rounds of a square park of side 70 m.
questions.solution_html:
Given that  length of a side = 70m
Perimeter of the square park = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} length of a side = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 70 m = 280 m
Distance covered in one round = 280 m
Therefore, distance travelled in three rounds = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 280m = 840m

questions.question_html: Pinky runs around a square field of side 75 m, Bob runs around a rectangular field with length 160 m and breadth 105 m. Who covers more distance and by how much?
questions.solution_html:
Given that, Distance covered by Pinky in one round = Perimeter of the square
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} length of a side
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 75 m = 300 m
Distance covered by Bob in one round = Perimeter of the rectangle
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (length + breadth)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (160 m + 105 m)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 265 m = 530 m
Difference in the distance covered = 530 m – 300 m = 230 m.
Thus, Bob covers more distance by 230 m.

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of a regular pentagon with each side measuring 3 cm.
questions.solution_html:
This regular closed pentagon has 5 sides, each with a length of 3 cm.
Therefore, Perimeter of the regular pentagon = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 cm = 15 cm

questions.question_html: The perimeter of a regular hexagon is 18 cm. How long is its one side?
questions.solution_html:
Given the Perimeter of regular hexagon = 18 cm
A regular hexagon has 6 sides, so we can divide the perimeter by 6 to get the length of one side.
One side of the hexagon = 18 cm {tex}\div{/tex} 6 = 3 cm
Therefore, the length of each side of the regular hexagon is 3 cm.

questions.question_html: Find the area of the shape shown in the figure.
questions.solution_html:
This figure is made up of line-segments. Moreover, it is covered by full squares and half squares only.
Here,
Fully-filled squares = 3
Half-filled squares = 3
If the area of one such square is taken to be as 1 square unit.
Area covered by full squares = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 sq units = 3 sq units.
Area covered by Half-filled squares = 3 x 0.5 = 1.5
Therefore, Total area = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5 sq units.

questions.question_html: By counting squares, estimate the area of the figure.
questions.solution_html:
From the above figure, we have,

Covered area  Number Area estimate (sq units)
(i) Fully-filled squares 11 11
(ii) Half-filled squares 3 {tex}3 \times \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
(iii) More than half-filled squares 7 7
(iv) Less than half-filled squares 5 0

Therefore, Total area = 11 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} + 7 = 19 {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sq units.

questions.question_html: By counting squares, estimate the area of the figure.
questions.solution_html:
From the given figure, we have, 

Covered area Number Area area estimate (sq units)
Fully-filled squares 1 1
Half-filled squares - -
More than half-filled squares 7 7
Less than half-filled squares 9 0

Total area = 1 + 7 = 8 sq units.

questions.question_html: Find the area of a rectangle whose length and breadth are 12 cm and 4 cm respectively.
questions.solution_html:
Given that,
Length of the rectangle = 12 cm
Breadth of the rectangle = 4 cm
Therefore, Area of the rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth
= 12 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 4 cm = 48 sq cm

questions.question_html: Find the area of a square plot of side 8 m.
questions.solution_html:
Given that side of the square = 8 m
Therefore, Area of the square = side {tex}\times{/tex} side
= 8 m {tex}\times{/tex} 8 m = 64 sq m.

questions.question_html: The area of a rectangular piece of cardboard is 36 sq cm and its length is 9 cm. What is the width of the cardboard?
questions.solution_html:
Given:
Area of the rectangle = 36 sq cm
Length = 9 cm
Width = ?
Area of a rectangle = length {tex}\times{/tex} width
So, width = {tex}\frac{\text { Area }}{\text { Length }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{36}{9}{/tex} = 4 cm
Therefore, the width of the rectangular cardboard is 4 cm.

questions.question_html: Bob wants to cover the floor of a room 3 m wide and 4 m long by squared tiles. If each square tile is of side 0.5 m, then find the number of tiles required to cover the floor of the room.
questions.solution_html:
Given:
Length of the room = 4 m
Breadth of the room = 3 m
Area of the floor = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth
= 4 m {tex}\times{/tex} 3 m = 12 sq m
Area of one square tile = side {tex}\times{/tex} side
= 0.5 m {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 m
= 0.25 sq m
Number of tiles required = {tex}\frac{Area\ of \ the floor}{Area \ of \ one \ tile}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{12}{0.25}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1200}{25}{/tex} = 48 tiles

questions.question_html: Find the area in a square metre of a piece of cloth 1m 25 cm wide and 2 m long.
questions.solution_html:
Given:
Length of the cloth = 2 m
Breadth of the cloth = 1 m 25 cm = 1 m + 0. 25 m = 1.25 m (since 25 cm = 0.25m)
Therefore, Area of the cloth = length of the cloth {tex}\times{/tex} breadth of the cloth
= 2 m {tex}\times{/tex} 1.25 m = 2.50 sq m

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of the figure:
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
Perimeter of a polygon = Sum of the lengths of all sides of that polygon
Here, we have:
Perimeter = (4 + 2 + 1 + 5) cm = 12 cm
Therefore, the perimeter is 12 cm

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of the figure:
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
The perimeter of a polygon = Sum of the lengths of all sides of that polygon
In this question, we have:
Perimeter = 4 (1 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 3) cm
= 52 cm

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of the figure:
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
The perimeter of a polygon = Sum of the lengths of all sides of that polygon
In this question, we have:
Perimeter = 2(4 + 0.5 + 2.5)+1 cm
= 15 cm

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of the figure:
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
Perimeter of a polygon = Sum of the lengths of all sides of that polygon
Here, we have:
Perimeter = (23 + 35 + 40 + 35) cm
= 133 cm

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of the figure:
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
Perimeter of a polygon = Sum of the lengths of all sides of that polygon
Here, we have:
Perimeter = (4 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 4) cm
= 20 cm

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of the figure:
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
Perimeter of a polygon = Sum of the lengths of all sides of that polygon
Here, we have:
Perimeter = (15 + 15 + 15 + 15) cm
= 60 cm

questions.question_html: The lid of a rectangular box of sides 40 cm and 10 cm is sealed all round with tape. What is the length of the tape required?
questions.solution_html:
Length of the tape required = Perimeter of the rectangular box
= 2 × (Length + Breadth)
= 2 × (40 cm + 10 cm) = 2 × (50 cm)
= 100 cm = 1 m

questions.question_html: Table-Top measures 2 m 25 cm by 1 m 50 cm. What is the perimeter of the table-top?
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of the table-top
= 2 × (Length + Breadth)
= 2 × (2 m 25 cm + 1 m 50 cm)
= 2 × (2.25 m + 1.50 m) = 2 × (3.75 m)
= 7.50 m

questions.question_html: What is the length of the wooden strip required to frame a photograph of length and breadth 32 cm and 21 cm, respectively?
questions.solution_html:
Length of the wooden strip required
= 2 × (Length + Breadth)
= 2 × (32 cm + 21 cm) = 2 × (53 cm)
= 106 cm = 1.06 m

questions.question_html: A rectangular piece of land measures 0.7 km by 0.5 km. Each side is to be fenced with 4 rows of wires. What is the length of the wire needed ?
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of the rectangle
= 2 × (Length + Breadth)
= 2 × (0.7 km + 0.5 km) = 2 × (1.2 km)
= 2.4 km
Length of the wire needed
= 4 × Perimeter of the rectangle
= 4 × (2.4 km)
= 9.6 km

questions.question_html:
Find the perimeter of the shape:
A triangle of sides 3 cm, 4 cm, and 5 cm.
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
The perimeter of a triangle = Sum of all sides
= (3 + 4 + 5) cm
= 12 cm

questions.question_html:
Find the perimeter of the shape:
An equilateral triangle of side 9 cm.
questions.solution_html:
We know that an equilateral triangle has all sides equal.
Thus, Perimeter of an equilateral triangle = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} Side of triangle
= (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 9) cm
= 27 cm

questions.question_html:
Find the perimeter of the shape:
An isosceles triangle with equal sides 8 cm each and third side 6 cm.
questions.solution_html:
We know in isosceles triangle two sides are equal.
So,
Perimeter = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 8) + 6
= 16 + 6
= 22 cm

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of a triangle with sides measuring 10 cm, 14 cm and 15 cm.
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of the triangle = Sum of the length of its three sides
= 10 cm + 14 cm + 15 cm
= 39 cm

questions.question_html: Find the perimeter of a regular hexagon with each side measuring 8 m.
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of the regular hexagon = 6 × length of a side
= 6 × (8 m)
= 48 m

questions.question_html: Find the side of the square whose perimeter is 20 m.
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of the square
= 4 × Length of a side
∴ Length of one side = {tex}\frac{\text { Perimeter of the square }}{4}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{20}{4} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
= 5 m

questions.question_html: The perimeter of a regular pentagon is 100 centimeter. How long is its each side?
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of the regular pentagon = 5 × Length of a side
∴ Length of one side ={tex}\frac{\text { Perimeter of the regular pentagon }}{5}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{100}{5} \mathrm{cm}{/tex}
= 20 cm

questions.question_html: A piece of string is 30 cm long. What will be the length of each side if the string is used to form a square?
questions.solution_html:
Given:
Perimeter of square = 30 cm
We know that,
The perimeter of square = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Side
30 = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Side
Side = {tex}\frac{30}{4}{/tex}
= 7.5 cm
Hence, the side of a square is 7.5 cm

questions.question_html: A piece of string is 30 cm long. What will be the length of each side if the string is used to form an equilateral triangle?
questions.solution_html:
Given that piece of string is used to form an equilateral triangle.
Hence,
Perimeter of equilateral triangle = 30 cm
We know that,
Perimeter of equilateral triangle = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} Side
30 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} Side
Side = {tex}\frac{30}{3}{/tex}
= 10 cm
Therefore, side of equilateral triangle is 10 cm

questions.question_html: A piece of string is 30 cm long. What will be the length of each side if the string is used to form a regular hexagon?
questions.solution_html:
Here, we have,
The perimeter of a regular hexagon = 30 cm
Now,
We know that,
The perimeter of regular hexagon = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} side
30 = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} Side
Side = {tex}\frac{30}{6}{/tex}
= 5 cm
Therefore, the side of the regular hexagon is 5 cm.

questions.question_html: Two sides of a triangle are 12 cm and 14 cm. The perimeter of the triangle is 36 cm. What is the third side?
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of a triangle = Sum of the lengths of its three sides
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 36 cm = 12 cm + 14 cm + Length of the third side
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 36 cm = 26 cm + Length of the third side
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Length of the third side = 36 cm – 26 cm = 10 cm

questions.question_html: Find the coast of fencing a square park of side 250 m at the rate of Rs 20 per metre.
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of the square park
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Length of a side
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (250 m)
= 1000 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of fencing the square park at the rate of Rs 20 per metre
= Rs 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 20
= Rs. 20000

questions.question_html: Find the cost of fencing a rectangular park of length 175 m and breadth 125 m at the rate of ₹ 12 per metre.
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of the rectangular park
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (Length + Breadth)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (175 m + 125m)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (300 m)
= 600 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of fencing the rectangular park at the rate of Rs 12 per meter
= Rs 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 12
= Rs 7200

questions.question_html: Sweety runs around a square park of side 75 m. Bulbul runs around a rectangular with length 60 m and breadth 45 m. Who covers less distance?
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter of the square park
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Length of a side
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} (75 m)
= 300 m
Perimeter of the rectangular park
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (length + Breadth)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (60 m + 45 m)
= 210 m
Since, the perimeter of the rectangular park is less than the perimeter of the square park, therefore, Bulbul covers less distance.

questions.question_html: What is the perimeter of the figure? What do you infer from the answer:
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter
= Sum of the length of all the sides
= 25 cm + 25 cm + 25 cm + 25 cm
= 100 cm
This shows that it is a square because all sides of a square are equal.

questions.question_html: What is the perimeter of the figure? What do you infer from the answer?
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter
= Sum of the lengths of all the sides
= 30 cm + 20 cm + 30 cm + 20 cm
= 100 cm
This is a rectangle because its opposite sides are of equal length.

questions.question_html: What is the perimeter of the figure given below? What do you infer from the figure?
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter
= Sum of the lengths of all the sides
= 40 cm + 10 cm + 40 cm + 10 cm
= 100 cm
This is a rectangle because its opposite sides are equal.

questions.question_html: What is the perimeter of the figure? What do you infer from the figure?
questions.solution_html:
Perimeter
= Sum of the lengths of all the sides
= 30 cm + 40 cm + 30 cm
= 100 cm
This is a triangle because it has three sides.

questions.question_html:
Avneet buys 9 square paving slabs, each with a side of {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} m. He lays them in the form of a square.

  1. What is the perimeter of his arrangement [Fig.]?
  2. Shari does not like his arrangement. She gets him to lay them out like a cross. What is the perimeter of her arrangement [(Fig.]?
  3. Which has greater perimeter?
  4. Avneet wonders if there is a way of getting an even greater perimeter. Can you find a way of doing this? (The paving slabs must meet along complete edges i.e. they cannot be broken.)
questions.solution_html:
1. From the given figure, the side of one slab is {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} m

    Therefore, the side of the square formed by Avneet {tex}=\left(3 \times \frac{1}{2}\right) m=\frac{3}{2} m{/tex}
    Now,
    We know that the perimeter of a square = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} Side {tex}=4 \times \frac{3}{2}=6 \mathrm{m}{/tex}
  2. We know that,
    Perimeter = Sum of all sides

    Therefore Perimeter of the Shari's arrangement = 0.5 + 1 + 1 + 0.5 + 1 + 1 + 0.5 + 1 + 1 + 0.5 + 1 + 1 = 10 m
  3. The arrangement which is in the shape of the cross has a greater perimeter (i.e. 10 m)
  4. If we put all 9 slabs in a line then, the perimeter will be 10 m, as shown below.

    Thus, from the given arrangements, arrangements with perimeters greater than 10 m cannot be determined.
    Hence 10 is the largest possible value if the perimeter.

questions.question_html: Find the area of the figure by counting squares:
questions.solution_html:
Full-filled squares = 2
Half-filled squares = 4
Area covered by full squares = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 sq units = 2 sq units
Area covered by half squares = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sq units = 2 sq units
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total Area = 2 sq units + 2 sq units = 4 sq units

questions.question_html: Find the area of the figure by counting squares:

half-filled squares = 4

Area covered by half squares = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} sq unit = 2 sq units
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total Area = 2 sq units + 2 sq units = 4 sq units

half-filled squares = 4

questions.question_html: Find the area of the figure by counting squares:

questions.question_html: Find the area of the figure by counting squares:
questions.solution_html:
By observing the figure, we get

Covered Area Total Numbers Area
Fully filled squares 9 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = 9

More than half-filled squares 9 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 9 = 9

questions.question_html: Find the area of the rectangle whose side are 3 cm and 4 cm.
questions.solution_html: Area of the rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 cm = 12 sq cm

questions.question_html: Find the area of the rectangle whose side are 12 m and 21 m.
questions.solution_html: Area of the rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth = 12 m {tex}\times{/tex} 21 m = 252 sq m

questions.question_html: Find the area of the rectangle whose side are 2 km and 3 km.
questions.solution_html: Area of the rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth = 2 km {tex}\times{/tex} 3 km = 6 sq km

questions.question_html: Find the area of the rectangle whose side are 2 m and 70 cm.
questions.solution_html:
1 m = 100 cm
2 m = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 cm = 200 cm
Area of the rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 70 sq cm = 14,000 sq cm

questions.question_html: Find the area of the square whose side are 10 cm.
questions.solution_html:
Area of the square = side {tex}\times{/tex} side
= 10 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 10 cm = 100 sq cm

questions.question_html: Find the area of the square whose side are 14 cm.

questions.question_html: Find the area of the square whose side are 5 m.

questions.question_html:
. The length and breadth of the three rectangles are as given below:

  1. 9 m and 6 m
  2. 3 m and 17 m
  3. 4 m and 14 m

Which one has the largest area and which one has the smallest?
questions.solution_html:
1. Area of the rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
    = 9 m {tex}\times{/tex} 6 m
    = 54 sq m
  2. Length of the rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
    = 3 m {tex}\times{/tex} 17 m
    = 51 sq m
  3. Length of the rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
    = 4 m {tex}\times{/tex} 14 m
    = 56 sq m

The rectangle (iii) has the largest area and the rectangle (ii) has the smallest area.

questions.question_html: The area of a rectangular garden 50 m long is 300 sq. m. Find the width of the garden.
questions.solution_html:
Area of the rectangular garden = 300 sq m
Length of the rectangular garden = 50 m
∴ Width of the rectangular garden
{tex}=\frac{\text { Area of the rectangular garden }}{\text { Length of the rectangular garden }}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{300}{50} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
= 6 m

questions.question_html: What is the cost of tiling a rectangular piece of land 500 m long and 200 m wide at the rate of Rs 8 per hundred sq m?
questions.solution_html:
Length of the rectangular piece of land = 500 m
Breadth of the rectangular piece of land = 200 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangular piece of land = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
= 500 m {tex}\times{/tex} 200 m
= 100000 sq m
{tex}\because{/tex} Cost of tilling 100 sq m = Rs 8
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of tilling 1 sq m = Rs {tex}\frac{8}{100}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of tilling 100000 sq m = Rs  {tex}\frac{8}{100} \times 100000{/tex}
= Rs 8,000

questions.question_html: A table-top measures 2 m by 1 m 50 cm. What is its area in square metres?
questions.solution_html:
Length of the table-top = 2 m
Breadth of the table-top = 1 m 50 cm = 1.50 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the table-top = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth = 2 m {tex}\times{/tex} 1.50 m
= 3.0 sq m

questions.question_html: A room is 4 m long and 3 m 50 cm wide. How many square metre of carpet is needed to cover the floor of the room?
questions.solution_html:
Length of the room = 4 m
Breadth of the room = 3 m 50 cm
= 3.50 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the room = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
= 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.5 sq. m
= 14.0 sq m
Hence, 14.0 square metres of carpet is needed to cover the floor of the room.

questions.question_html: A floor is 5 m long and 4 m wide. A square carpet of sides 3 m is laid on the floor. Find the area of the floor that is not carpeted.
questions.solution_html:
Length of the floor = 5 m
Breadth of the floor = 4 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the floor = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
= 5 m {tex}\times{/tex} 4 m
= 20 sq m
Area of the square carpet = side {tex}\times{/tex} side
= 3 m {tex}\times{/tex} 3 m
= 9 sq m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the floor that is not carpeted = 20 sq m – 9 sq m
= 11 sq m.

questions.question_html: Five square flower beds each of sides 1 m are dug on a piece of land 5 m long and 4 m wide. What is the area of the remaining part of land?
questions.solution_html:
Area of square flower bed = {tex}\times{/tex} side
= 1 m {tex}\times{/tex} 1 m
= 1 sq m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of 5 square flower beds = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 sq m
= 5 sq m
Length of the piece of land = 5 m
Breadth of the piece of land = 4 m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the piece of land = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
= 5 m {tex}\times{/tex} 4 m
= 20 sq m
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the remaining part of land = 20 sq m – 5 sq m
= 15 sq m

questions.question_html: By splitting the figure into rectangle, find out the area. (The measures are given in centimetre).
questions.solution_html:
Area of the figure = (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 + 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) sq cm
= (9 + 2 + 9 + 8) sq cm
= 28 sq cm

questions.question_html: By splitting the figure into rectangle, find their area.(The measures are given in centimetre).
questions.solution_html:
Area of the figure = (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) sq cm
= (3 + 3 + 3) sq cm
= 9 sq cm

questions.question_html: Split the shape into a rectangle and find its area. (The measures are given in centimetres)
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
Area of rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
Hence, from the figure,
Area of 1st rectangle = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 24 cm2
Also,
Area of 2nd rectangle = 8 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 16 cm2
Thereore,
Total area = 24 + 16 cm2 = 40 cm2

questions.question_html: Split the shape into a rectangle and find its area. (The measures are given in centimetres)
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
Area of rectangle = Length × Breadth
Also,
Area of square = (Side)2
Therefore,
Area of one square = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 = 49 cm2
As there are 5 squares in a total of equal size.
Therefore,
Area of 5 squares = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 49 cm2 = 245 cm2

questions.question_html: Split the shape into a rectangle and find its area. (The measures are given in centimetres)
questions.solution_html:
We know that,
Area of rectangle = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
Therefore,
From the figure, we have,
Area of 1st recatngle = 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 5 cm2
Also,
Area of 2nd rectangle = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4 cm2
Therefore,
Total area = 5 + 4 = 9 cm2

questions.question_html: How many tiles whose length and breadth are 12 cm and 5 cm respectively will be needed to fit in a rectangular region whose length and breadth are 100 cm and 144 cm respectively.
questions.solution_html:
Length of the region = 100 cm
Breadth of the region = 144 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the region = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
= 100 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 144cm
= 14400 sq cm
Length of a tile = 12 cm
Breadth of a tile = 5 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of a tile = Length {tex}\times{/tex} Breadth
= 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 sq cm
= 60 sq cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of tiles needed to fit the region
  {tex}=\frac{\text { Area of the region }}{\text { Area of a tile }}{/tex}
={tex}\frac{14400}{60}{/tex} = 240

questions.question_html: How many tiles whose length and breadth are 12 cm and 5 cm respectively will be needed to fit in a rectangular region whose length and breadth are 70 cm and 36 cm respectively.
questions.solution_html:
Length of the region = 70 cm
Breadth of the region = 36 cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangular region = 70 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 36 cm
= 2520 sq cm
Area of a tile = 12 cm {tex}\times{/tex} 5 cm = 60 sq cm
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Number if tiles needed to fit the region
{tex}=\frac{\text { Area of the region }}{\text { Area of a tile }}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{2520}{60}{/tex}
= 42
